clues.name: My name comes from the Greek for ‘to chew.’
clues.function: I elevate the mandible during jaw closure.
clues.innervation: I am supplied by the mandibular division of the trigeminal nerve.
clues.origin: I arise in part from the zygomatic arch.
clues.insertion: I attach along the ramus and angle of the mandible.
clues.clinical: Clenching the teeth makes me easy to palpate at the lateral jaw.

feedback.correct: The masseter elevates the mandible and is a powerful muscle of mastication.
feedback.review: Look for the thick vertical muscle spanning the zygomatic arch to the mandibular ramus.

clues.function: I produce serous saliva that reaches the mouth near the second upper molar.
clues.anatomicalRelationship: The facial nerve branches within me but does not innervate my secretion.
clues.innervation: My parasympathetic secretomotor fibers originate with cranial nerve IX.
clues.clinical: Inflammation here can cause painful swelling anterior to the ear.

feedback.correct: The parotid is the largest major salivary gland and sits anterior and inferior to the ear.
feedback.review: Place it in the preauricular region, posterior to the mandibular ramus.

clues.function: I flex the elbow and powerfully supinate the forearm.
clues.insertion: My tendon attaches to the radial tuberosity.
clues.origin: My two heads arise from the coracoid process and supraglenoid tubercle.
clues.dysfunction: My distal tendon rupture may produce a ‘Popeye’ deformity and weak supination.
clues.anatomicalRelationship: My tendon crosses the anterior elbow before reaching the radius.

feedback.correct: Biceps brachii flexes and supinates the forearm, inserting on the radial tuberosity.
feedback.review: Follow the anterior arm muscle distally across the elbow toward the proximal radius.

clues.function: My motor branches extend the wrist and fingers.
clues.dysfunction: Injury to me may produce wrist drop.
clues.anatomicalRelationship: I wind around the posterior humerus in the radial groove.
clues.clinical: A midshaft humeral fracture puts me at risk.
clues.innervation: I am the terminal continuation of the posterior cord of the brachial plexus.

feedback.correct: The radial nerve courses posteriorly around the humerus before dividing near the lateral elbow.
feedback.review: Trace a posterior-arm pathway toward the lateral side of the elbow.

clues.function: I dorsiflex and invert the foot.
clues.origin: I arise from the lateral condyle and upper lateral tibia.
clues.insertion: I reach the medial cuneiform and base of the first metatarsal.
clues.innervation: The deep fibular nerve supplies me.
clues.clinical: Weakness contributes to foot drop and a high-stepping gait.

feedback.correct: Tibialis anterior occupies the anterior leg and is a key dorsiflexor.
feedback.review: Place it immediately lateral to the tibial crest in the anterior compartment.

clues.anatomicalRelationship: I curve around the neck of the fibula.
clues.dysfunction: Compression of me can cause foot drop.
clues.pathway: I divide into superficial and deep fibular branches.
clues.clinical: A tight cast near the lateral knee may injure me.
clues.function: My branches supply dorsiflexors, evertors, and sensation over much of the dorsum of the foot.

feedback.correct: The common fibular nerve wraps superficially around the fibular neck before branching.
feedback.review: Find the short curved nerve pathway at the lateral knee and proximal fibula.

clues.function: My fibers elevate, retract, depress, and upwardly rotate the scapula.
clues.innervation: My motor supply is the spinal accessory nerve.
clues.origin: I arise from the occiput, nuchal ligament, and spinous processes to T12.
clues.insertion: I attach to the clavicle, acromion, and spine of the scapula.
clues.clinical: Weakness after neck surgery can produce shoulder droop.

feedback.correct: Trapezius is a broad superficial muscle spanning the posterior neck and upper back.
feedback.review: Center the broad diamond-shaped muscle between the occiput, shoulders, and lower thoracic midline.

clues.function: I provide motor supply to sternocleidomastoid and trapezius.
clues.dysfunction: My injury causes weak shoulder shrug and difficulty turning the head away.
clues.anatomicalRelationship: I cross the posterior triangle of the neck superficially.
clues.clinical: I am vulnerable during lymph-node biopsy in the posterior triangle.
clues.pathway: My spinal rootlets ascend through the foramen magnum before I exit the jugular foramen.

feedback.correct: Cranial nerve XI crosses the posterior triangle to reach trapezius.
feedback.review: Trace the superficial oblique pathway from the upper neck toward the shoulder.

clues.pathway: Nasal retinal fibers cross within me.
clues.function: I help each cerebral hemisphere receive information from the opposite visual field.
clues.anatomicalRelationship: I lie just superior to the pituitary gland.
clues.imaging: A sellar mass may elevate or compress me on midline imaging.

feedback.correct: The optic chiasm is the X-shaped crossing of nasal retinal fibers above the pituitary.
feedback.review: Place the crossing on the midline at the anterior base of the brain.

clues.function: I carry voluntary motor commands from cortex toward spinal motor neurons.
clues.pathway: Most of my fibers decussate in the caudal medulla.
clues.dysfunction: A lesion above my crossing causes contralateral upper motor neuron signs.
clues.anatomicalRelationship: I pass through the posterior limb of the internal capsule and cerebral peduncles.
clues.clinical: Damage may produce weakness, hyperreflexia, and an extensor plantar response.

feedback.correct: The corticospinal tract descends from motor cortex through the brainstem toward the spinal cord.
feedback.review: Align the descending motor pathway through the cerebral peduncle and ventral brainstem.

clues.function: I generate pressure to propel blood through pulmonary and systemic circuits.
clues.anatomicalRelationship: Most of me lies left of the midline within the middle mediastinum.
clues.development: My primordial tube loops rightward early in development.
clues.imaging: My silhouette forms much of the left cardiomediastinal contour on a frontal radiograph.
clues.surfaceLandmark: My apex beat is typically found near the left fifth intercostal space at the midclavicular line.

feedback.correct: The heart sits obliquely in the middle mediastinum with its apex directed down and left.
feedback.review: Center it between the lungs and rotate the apex toward the patient’s left.

clues.function: I provide the diaphragm’s motor innervation.
clues.origin: My roots arise mainly from C3, C4, and C5.
clues.anatomicalRelationship: I descend anterior to the lung root, between mediastinal pleura and pericardium.
clues.dysfunction: Unilateral injury may elevate one hemidiaphragm.
clues.clinical: I carry sensory fibers from central diaphragmatic pleura and pericardium.

feedback.correct: The phrenic nerve descends on the pericardium anterior to the lung root.
feedback.review: Trace a nearly vertical nerve along the lateral pericardium toward the diaphragm.

clues.function: I store and concentrate bile between meals.
clues.anatomicalRelationship: I rest in a fossa on the visceral surface of the liver.
clues.substance: I do not make bile, but I concentrate it by absorbing water.
clues.clinical: A stone blocking my outlet may cause right upper-quadrant pain after a fatty meal.
clues.surfaceLandmark: My fundus projects near the right ninth costal cartilage at the lateral rectus border.

feedback.correct: The gallbladder lies tucked beneath the liver in the right upper abdomen.
feedback.review: Place the pear-shaped sac on the visceral surface of the right liver.

clues.function: Peristaltic waves move urine through me from kidney to bladder.
clues.anatomicalRelationship: I cross the pelvic brim near the bifurcation of the common iliac vessels.
clues.histology: My lumen is lined by urothelium and often appears stellate in section.
clues.clinical: A stone can lodge where I cross the pelvic brim or enter the bladder wall.
clues.development: I arise from the ureteric bud.

feedback.correct: The ureter descends retroperitoneally and crosses the pelvic brim en route to the bladder.
feedback.review: Align the narrow tube from the posterior abdomen across the pelvic brim toward the bladder.
